ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for red dates stuffed with glutinous rice; closest match is glutinous rice food product (FOODON_03315671). Red date (jujube) component not separately anchored in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (rice-based dishes) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for red dates stuffed with glutinous rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific traditional Chinese sweet dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of red dates with glutinous rice consists of 3 to 4 stuffed red dates, weighing approximately 100 g, often served warm as a dessert or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Red Dates With Glutinous Rice (心太软) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 红枣圆 Red Dates With Glutinous Rice (G00010048). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 0.8 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for glutinous rice and jujube components (USDA SR Legacy #20052 and #09183).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for red dates stuffed with glutinous rice. GI estimated at 86 based on glutinous rice (white, cooked) GI of 86–98 as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dominant carbohydrate contributor is glutinous rice (waxy rice), which has a very high GI due to its near-100% amylopectin starch content. Red dates contribute dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) which modestly attenuates the GI; a conservative estimate of 86 is used. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = GI × carbohydrate_g / 100 = 86 × 25.19 / 100 = 21.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g (borderline high); partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g per DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving (approximately 100 g) and request the dish plain or with minimal syrup, as added syrup increases caloric and sugar load without reducing sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice has a very high GI (~86–98) due to near-100% amylopectin content, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; carbohydrate 25.2 g/100g with glycemic load ~21.7 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish to very small portions (≤50 g) and pair it with low-GI foods, as the glutinous rice base causes rapid and significant blood glucose spikes that are difficult to manage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g is borderline; saturated fat is low (~0.8 g/100g) and cholesterol is zero; red dates provide polyphenols and iron; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g supports cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish is acceptable in small portions given its low saturated fat and zero cholesterol, but sodium intake should be monitored and syrup-based preparations avoided to prevent excess sugar and caloric load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red dates are rich in polyphenols and antioxidants with documented anti-inflammatory properties; dish is steamed with no AGE-generating high-heat cooking; negligible saturated fat and zero trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed in moderate portions without added syrup, as the anti-inflammatory polyphenols in red dates may be beneficial, but high glycaemic load from glutinous rice should be limited to avoid indirect pro-inflammatory effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Dates with Glutinous Rice (红枣圆, also known as 心太软 Xīn Tài Ruǎn, meaning 'heart too soft') is a traditional Chinese sweet dish popular in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The dish consists of whole dried red dates (jujube, Ziziphus jujuba) that are pitted and stuffed with a soft, chewy glutinous rice (waxy rice) filling, then steamed until tender. The texture contrasts the slightly wrinkled, sweet-savoury skin of the red date with the sticky, yielding glutinous rice centre. The flavour profile is mildly sweet with an earthy, slightly tangy note from the jujube. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (of which approximately 0.8 g is saturated), 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, 231 mg sodium, 327 mg potassium, 54 mg calcium, 40 mg magnesium, and 2.55 mg iron. Cholesterol is zero. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 86 (high) due to the near-100% amylopectin starch in glutinous rice, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes in regular or large portions. The dietary fibre from red dates provides modest cardiovascular and gut health benefits. Red dates are a recognised source of polyphenols, vitamin C, and antioxidants with anti-inflammatory properties, making the dish potentially beneficial for individuals with inflammatory conditions when consumed without added syrup. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. It is commonly served as a dessert or snack at lunch and dinner, and is associated with Chinese festive and everyday dining contexts in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore dishes, record G00010048) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for glutinous rice and jujube. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for glutinous rice (waxy rice, cooked). All mandatory multilingual names present for Singaporean Chinese dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this traditional steamed preparation is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010048) or USDA FoodData Central composite entry as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB-reported fields is not available in the primary source for this dish as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — this dish is steamed from plant-based ingredients with no hydrogenated fats; trans fat is naturally absent. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source; dish contains no animal-derived fat. sugar_g is 0.21 g/100g as reported by HPB; this value appears low for a red date preparation and may underrepresent natural fruit sugars — consumers and clinicians should note that actual sugar content may be higher depending on preparation and syrup addition. Saturated fat of 0.8 g/100g is an estimate derived from USDA SR Legacy #20052 (glutinous rice, cooked) and #09183 (jujube, raw) component proportions, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
